dominant_error_pattern: Students misidentify the emotional processing hub, selecting unrelated cortical or speech areas instead of the limbic amygdala, showing confusion between language regions and emotion circuits.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B (Broca's area) confuses language production with emotion; distractor C (prefrontal cortex) conflates executive function with feeling. Clarify: amygdala is the emotional 'alarm bell' in the limbic system, generating fear and emotional memory. Use amygdala-lesion patients who cannot recognize fear in faces.

dominant_error_pattern: Students underestimate TBI severity in children, wrongly claiming neuroplasticity guarantees full recovery or overgeneralizing specific neuropsych profiles that do not fit all TBI outcomes.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B (less serious than adults) and C (always impacts cognition) both mismatch child neurobiology with TBI reality. Teach: children's greater neuroplasticity is a *benefit* but not immunity; TBI still causes lasting learning, attention, and behavioral sequelae. Emphasize individual variability in recovery based on injury site, severity, and age at injury.

dominant_error_pattern: Students misidentify the foundational neurocognitive process underlying reading fluency, conflating reasoning (higher-order) with basic processing speed or selecting non-linguistic domains.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A (nonverbal reasoning) and B (verbal reasoning) both name higher-order processes, not basic ones. Distinguish: processing speed = basic neural efficiency (how fast you retrieve/process info); reasoning = complex synthesis (how you problem-solve). Show that fluency requires speed first, then comprehension builds on that base.

dominant_error_pattern: Students fail to connect executive function correlates to the specific brain region executing those functions, instead selecting lobes associated with sensory input or memory.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A (occipital lobe) misattributes EF to vision; distractor C (global function) avoids the localization question. Emphasize frontal lobe's monopoly on initiation, impulse control, organization, flexibility, and attention. Use executive dysfunction case studies (frontal lobe patients) to show the behavioral signature of frontal damage.

dominant_error_pattern: Students over-generalize left-hemisphere functions to damage cases or deny right-hemisphere contributions to nonverbal and novel reasoning, missing the complementary specialization.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A (memory for prior skills) invokes left-hemisphere strength; distractor B (phonologic processing) is also left-dominant. The right hemisphere excels at spatial reasoning, novel problem-solving, and holistic processing. Use split-brain patients: right hemisphere (mute) solves spatial/novel tasks its verbal left side cannot express.

dominant_error_pattern: Students either minimize concussion's symptom profile to headache alone or overestimate it to severe motor/speech deficits, failing to recognize the visual-vestibular-headache triad of occipital/parietal impact.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B (headache only) undershoots symptom breadth; distractor D (talking/walking deficits) overstates severity for mild TBI. Teach the occipital lobe concussion signature: visual processing disruption (light sensitivity, reading trouble), vestibular dizziness, and headache. Emphasize school support (rest breaks, lighting adjustment) over home isolation for mild concussions.
